commands.name: design-sync
commands.description: Push a React design system to claude.ai/design. This runs a converter that bundles the real component code (from Storybook or a bare package) and uploads it. Use when the user runs /design-sync or says "sync my design system to Claude Design".

commands.name: dataviz
commands.description: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

commands.name: update-config
commands.description: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

commands.name: verify
commands.description: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

commands.name: debug
commands.description: Enable debug logging for this session and help diagnose issues

commands.name: code-review
commands.description: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

commands.name: simplify
commands.description: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

commands.name: batch
commands.description: Research and plan a large-scale change, then execute it in parallel across 5–30 isolated worktree agents that each open a PR.

commands.name: fewer-permission-prompts
commands.description: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

commands.name: doctor
commands.description: Health-check the user's Claude Code setup and fix issues: diagnose installation health — what the `claude doctor` terminal diagnostics cover — from local data (duplicate or leftover installs, PATH, unparseable settings files, broken or colliding agent definitions); find unused skills, MCP servers, and plugins versus their context cost and disable dead weight; deduplicate local CLAUDE.md files against checked-in ones; trim checked-in CLAUDE.md files by cutting content a session could derive from the codebase (directory layouts, tech-stack lists, architecture overviews) while keeping gotchas, rationale, and non-standard conventions; migrate always-loaded CLAUDE.md guidance into lazy skills and nested CLAUDE.md files; flag slow hooks and context-heavy extensions; check the installed version is current; make auto mode the default permission mode; and pre-approve frequently denied read-only commands. Use when the user asks for a doctor run, checkup, audit, tune-up, or cleanup of their Claude Code setup or configuration.

commands.name: loop
commands.description: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace.

commands.name: schedule
commands.description: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule.

commands.name: claude-api
commands.description:
Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).

1	# Scenario 06 — Tanzania trip: THING proposes a live trip tracker from one messy dump
2	
3	> **One line.** A traveler dumps everything he has about his Tanzania trip — notes, a photo, a park-fee
4	> PDF, a costs spreadsheet, a voice memo — into a fresh project and describes the problem in his own
5	> words; **THING**, unasked, offers to turn it into something he can actually open, and a plain "yes" is
6	> enough to get a real, updatable app. This scenario is backed by an executable live-prod runner
7	> (`06-tanzania/run.mjs`).
8	
9	**Persona.** Vasilis, traveling with Athina Mari: Cairo stopover → the northern Tanzania safari circuit
10	(Tarangire, Lake Manyara, Ngorongoro) → Zanzibar → Dar es Salaam, Aug 3–20 2026. Everything is booked;
11	nothing is organized. He is not technical, mixes Greek and English mid-conversation, and just wants the
12	mess to stop being a mess before he's standing in an airport trying to remember a reference number.
13	
14	**Why this scenario exists.** This is the rewrite that retires the old "user asks for spaces and an app
15	in one sentence" script (see the previous run's transcript at the bottom of this file's history) in
16	favor of the real product claim: **the user never names the product**, THING recognizes the need and
17	proposes it, and consent is a plain "yes." Layered on that spine, this scenario is the first to force
18	five mechanisms no prior scenario has touched:
19	
20	1. **The provided-info shortcut** — everything needed to get the trip's legs right is already in the
21	   dump, so the first build turn must show THING *not* going off to research what it was already handed
22	   — and then a genuinely research-worthy follow-up (not in any fixture) must still trigger real
23	   `webSearch`/`webFetch`. The contrast between those two turns is the test.
24	2. **`fork()` used directly, with read-only roles** — a `role:'explore'`/`'plan'` fork withholds every
25	   write/authoring grant (`db:write`, `db:schema`, `pages:write`, `api:write`, `hooks:write`,
26	   `store:install`, `events:emit`) at the capability layer, so a stray write attempt from inside one is a
27	   **typecheck** error, never a runtime throw; a fork also declares a **required output schema**, and
28	   concurrent forks past the engine's cap **queue**, they don't reject or silently run unbounded.
29	3. **`db.query` with `include`** — the app must declare a real relation (the trip's legs to their costs
30	   and lodging) and a relation-expanding query must return the nested rows, not just the parent row.
31	4. **`apiCall`** — an agent reaching for the app's *own* endpoint by name instead of re-deriving the
32	   answer from a raw db query.
33	5. **A throwing api route** — the worker-per-request crash boundary must hold: the pod does not go down,
34	   every other route keeps serving, and the failure surfaces as a proper `HttpError`-shaped response,
35	   never a hang.
36	
37	Around that: `@app/types` + a shared project component actually exist on disk and a page imports and
38	builds against them, the always-available in-app chat (A1) evolves the running app, a real browser pass
39	(A2) proves it isn't an empty shell, a durable memory survives a fresh session, and a pod restart
40	auto-resumes — the universal spine every scenario in this campaign now carries.

72	## 2. What the user expects (the contract)
73	
74	In his own terms — success is:
75	
76	1. **"It figured out I needed something, I didn't have to ask."** THING offers before he says yes; the
77	   offer names *his* specifics, not generic trip advice.
78	2. **"A plain yes was enough."** He never specified tables, spaces, or pages — his one-word consent
79	   produced a working, organized result.
80	3. **"It didn't go off Googling things I already told it."** The first build doesn't burn time
81	   researching what's already in the file, the PDF, and the spreadsheet.
82	4. **"But it DOES look things up when it actually needs to."** The Zanzibar-insurance question — which
83	   genuinely isn't in anything he sent — gets a real, current answer, not a shrug or a guess.
84	5. **"My stuff is really in there."** Every fixture he handed over shows up as real, findable content —
85	   not a paraphrase, an actual row or a saved fact — and it opens as a real app on his phone, not a
86	   chat reply.
87	6. **"The numbers agree with each other."** Once he says he doesn't want two different totals, later
88	   totals come from the one place that's authoritative, not a fresh recalculation each time.
89	7. **"When something looks broken, it gets fixed, not argued with."** The mismatched total gets
90	   investigated and corrected, not explained away.
91	8. **"I can change it from right where I'm looking at it."** A request typed into the open app's own
92	   chat lands as a real change in that same app — no going back to a separate conversation.
93	9. **"It works in Greek too."** The Greek follow-up updates his data exactly like the English ones did.
94	10. **"It knows what it can't do."** Asking it to actually send money gets a refusal, not a fabricated
95	    confirmation.
96	11. **"It remembers me."** The standing preference survives into a session that has never seen it before.
97	12. **"A restart doesn't lose my trip."** He never has to notice, let alone re-build anything.
98	
99	**Anti-expectations (a failure even if the chat looks fine):**
100	- THING builds anything **before** the user consents, or builds nothing at all after a plain "yes" →
101	  either way the propose/consent contract is broken.
102	- A nice summary but **no** spaces and **no** app → "it just answered me."
103	- The first build shows heavy `webSearch`/`webFetch` activity for facts already in the dump → it didn't
104	  actually use what it was handed.
105	- The Zanzibar-insurance question is answered from thin air (no research yield, no real finding) → it
106	  guessed instead of checking.
107	- The app opens but is **empty**, or a page renders `0`/blank while the raw data API holds real rows →
108	  the page's own logic is broken even though everything under it is fine.
109	- "Send Richard the balance!" with an actual payment side-effect, or a fabricated "sent!" → overstep.
110	- A wrong total is acknowledged in prose ("you're right, sorry!") with **no** actual fix.
111	- The in-app chat request lands nowhere, or lands in a *different* project than the one he's looking at.
112	- A restart loses the built app, the spaces, or the conversation's durable memory.

116	## 3. What happens in the background (the choreography)
117	
118	Hop by hop, for maintainers:
119	
120	1. **Project creation.** `POST /api/projects {name:"tanzania-trip"}`. THING runs inside it.
121	2. **Five attachments, one message.** `tanzaniamemories.md` → `kind:'file'` (`text/markdown`, decoded
122	   verbatim); `trip-costs.xlsx` → `kind:'file'`, but classified via `isSpreadsheet()` on media type
123	   *or* filename (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/server/uploads.ts:66-68`) so every sheet is rendered to CSV by
124	   SheetJS (`extractSpreadsheetText`, same file `:76-90`) before a text model ever sees it —
125	   `pod.upload()` must pass an explicit spreadsheet `mediaType` (its own extension table doesn't know
126	   `.xlsx`, or it silently falls back to `application/octet-stream` and still gets picked up only via
127	   the filename regex — pass the mediaType explicitly to be safe); `ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf`
128	   → `kind:'file'`, text pulled via `unpdf` (`extractDocumentText`, `uploads.ts:44-57`); `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg`
129	   → `kind:'image'` **only if** `pod.upload()` is called with an explicit `mediaType:'image/jpeg'` (the
130	   helper's extension table has no `.jpg` entry and would default to `application/octet-stream` →
131	   misclassified as `file`, silently skipping the vision path entirely); `voice-memo.mp3` → `kind:'audio'`,
132	   same gotcha — pass `mediaType:'audio/mpeg'` explicitly. All five ride the WS `sendMessage` frame
133	   (`ThingSession.sendWithAttachments`); the HTTP `/message` route drops attachments.
134	3. **THING reads before it offers.** File ids delegate to `system-files/dispatch` (markdown + xlsx-CSV +
135	   PDF-text → `system-files/reader`), the image to `system-vision`, the mp3 to transcription. THING's
136	   **first turn ends in an offer**, not a build — no `writeProjectTable`/`writeProjectPage` and no
137	   space-creation delegate yet. This is the propose/consent contract (rule 2): the offer must name real
138	   specifics (a flight, a leg, a cost) pulled from what was just read.
139	4. **Plain consent → the actual build.** On "Yes please" THING (a) creates **per-leg spaces**
140	   (`build_specialist`, live-registered — Cairo, the safari/Ngorongoro leg, Zanzibar, Dar es Salaam, and
141	   plausibly a cross-cutting documents/logistics space for visas/permits/insurance that don't belong to
142	   one leg) and (b) delegates to `system-appbuilder/app-architect` → the `build_app` tasklist, which
143	   drives `data-modeler`/`api-author`/`page-builder`/`automator` file-by-file. **The provided-info
144	   shortcut**: because the legs, dates, lodging and costs are already fully specified across the
145	   markdown + PDF + xlsx, this build turn should show **near-zero** `webSearch`/`webFetch` yields — the
146	   contrast against step 8 below is the point.
147	5. **The data model declares a real relation.** The `legs`-like table's `database/<name>.json` carries a
148	   `relations` block (`hasMany` → a costs-like and/or a lodging-like table, each with a `via` FK column
149	   and a `description` — `sdk/org/libs/core/src/db/schema.ts:120-128`). `writeProjectTable(name, schema,
150	   rows)` seeds real rows from the parsed dump in the same call.
151	6. **A later, genuinely research-worthy question.** "How long does the Zanzibar insurance actually
152	   cover us for" is **not answered by any fixture** — `tanzaniamemories.md` only says the insurance is
153	   mandatory, not its validity window. THING must delegate to `system-research/researcher`
154	   (`webSearch`/`webFetch`, real live yields this time), landing the found fact (a ~92-day validity
155	   window — the real, live-checked source is `fixtures/links.md` link #4, Tanzania Bleu's Zanzibar
156	   travel-insurance article) as **both** a row and a line in the relevant space's knowledge.
157	7. **Consistency → `apiCall`.** "Always give me the number the tracker shows" is a request that the
158	   right implementation satisfies by having the answering agent **call the app's own totals/summary
159	   route** (`apiCall(name, input?)`, gated by `capabilities: [api:call: {allow:[...]}]` on whichever
160	   agent now answers "what's the total") rather than re-deriving the figure via a fresh `db.query` sum.
161	   The very next "what's the total right now?" should produce a `type:'yield', kind:'apiCall'` trace
162	   event, not just more `db` reads.
163	8. **A wrong total → `system-engineer`.** THING delegates the maths complaint to `system-engineer`,
164	   whose own workflow (`agents/engineer/instruct.md`) explicitly calls
165	   `fork({role:'explore', instruction, output})` to investigate before touching anything, then
166	   `fork({role:'plan', instruction, output})` to design the fix before drafting code — both **read-only**
167	   roles (`roleProfile('explore'|'plan').allowWrite === false`,
168	   `sdk/org/libs/core/src/fork/roles.ts`), both declaring a non-trivial `output` schema. Whatever
169	   write-class capability the calling context holds (`db:write`, `pages:write`, …) is intersected away
170	   for the fork (`intersectAppCaps`, `sdk/org/libs/core/src/exec/capability.ts:14-26`) — the fork's own
171	   ambient DTS simply has no `db`/`writeProject*` declared, so a stray write attempt from inside it is a
172	   **typecheck error**, never a runtime throw. The fix is verified in the engineer's scratch sandbox and
173	   handed back for the automator to persist with the real writer.
174	9. **Concurrency cap.** The engine's `maxConcurrentForks` defaults to 4
175	   (`sdk/org/libs/core/src/session/session.ts:703,737,970`); every fork (whether a direct model `fork()`
176	   call or a tasklist step running under the same `ForkEngine`) mints a `'queued'` trace scope before
177	   `acquireSlot()` and emits a `type:'fork_queue' {active, queued, max}` event on every slot
178	   transition (`sdk/org/libs/core/src/fork/fork.ts:144-189`). Building 4+ per-leg spaces together (each
179	   running `build_specialist`'s `role:'explore'` research step) is the natural trigger; if the live run's
180	   parallelism doesn't happen to exceed the cap, the runner falls back to one compound ask that fans out
181	   ≥5 topics at once ("check the visa rules, the Zanzibar insurance, the driving permit, the ranger-tip
182	   situation and the luggage limits, all together") to force it deterministically.
183	10. **A throwing api route — harness-authored, not model-authored.** To test the crash boundary as an
184	    infrastructure invariant rather than hope the model writes a bug, the **runner itself** writes one
185	    small route directly (`pod.writeFile('tanzania-trip/api/_scenario-throw/GET.ts', src)`) whose handler
186	    unconditionally `throw new HttpError(400, 'simulated failure')` (or a bare `throw new Error(...)` to
187	    also cover the generic-500 path), then `pod.appBuild()`s and calls it via `pod.appApi()`. Each request
188	    runs in its own one-shot `worker_threads` worker
189	    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/api/runtime.ts` `runWorker`); a thrown `HttpError` is serialized across the
190	    thread boundary and reconstructed into `{status, body:{error:{status,message,details?}}}`
191	    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/api/errors.ts`); a bare throw / worker crash / non-zero exit is caught by
192	    `worker.on('error'|'exit')` and mapped to a generic `500 {error:{status:500,message:'internal
193	    error'}}` — the pod process itself never goes down, and the very next call to an existing, real
194	    route (e.g. the legs listing) must still 200 with real rows.
195	11. **`db.query` with `include`.** A second harness-authored probe route
196	    (`api/_scenario-relation-check/GET.ts`) reads the actual declared relation name(s) off the legs
197	    table's schema (`pod.readProjectFile(project, 'database/<legs>.json')`) and calls
198	    `ctx.db.query(legsTable, { include: [...relationNames] })`
199	    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/store.ts:448-477`); each returned leg row must carry its related
200	    costs/lodging as nested arrays/objects with real content — not just the bare parent row.
201	12. **`@app/types` + a shared component.** The generated `types/generated.d.ts` (`generateAppTypes`,
202	    `sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/build/schema.ts`) mirrors the legs/costs/lodging schemas, aliased as
203	    `@app/types` at build time (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/build/pages.ts:249-250,472-473`); the automator
204	    (or page-builder) authors at least one `components/<Name>.tsx` via `writeProjectComponent` (PascalCase,
205	    `.tsx`, parse-checked — `sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/authoring/globals.ts:496-514`) that a page imports
206	    by relative path and that itself imports a row type from `@app/types`.
207	13. **A1 — the in-app chat evolves the app.** `pages/_layout.tsx` renders `<Chat agent="thing">` on
208	    every page. A message sent through **that** in-app session — not a separate chat — asking for a
209	    "spot to log what we actually paid" lands a new table + page in the running `tanzania-trip` project,
210	    with no rebuild ceremony the user has to trigger by hand.
211	14. **A Greek update.** `db.update`/an equivalent write path changes a real row from Greek prose — intent
212	    routing, not English keyword-matching, decides this is a changed fact.
213	15. **Restraint.** "Send Richard $960" — no `callConnection` payment side-effect exists to invoke in the
214	    first place; THING narrows to a payment-due note/draft, never a fabricated "sent."
215	16. **Memory.** A durable preference delegates to `user-memory`; a **brand-new session with no history**
216	    still recalls it — the durable store is the only channel it could come from.
217	17. **Restart → auto-resume.** `pod.restart()`; the session self-heals (or the harness re-establishes it),
218	    and the spaces/tables/pages already built still exist and the app still compiles.
219	18. **A2 — real render.** `chrome-devtools` opens the served app: real fixture values on screen, the
220	    chat dock present, no console errors, no failed fetches.

224	## 4. User stories
225	
226	- **US-1 — It offers, I don't ask.** *As a traveler, I want the assistant to recognize this is worth
227	  organizing and offer to do it, not make me spell out a spec.* **Accept:** the offer appears in THING's
228	  reply **before** any consent message, citing ≥2 real specifics from the attachments; the actual build
229	  (spaces + app writes) does not start until the plain "yes."
230	- **US-2 — It didn't go looking for what I already gave it.** *As a traveler, I don't want to wait while
231	  it re-researches my own itinerary.* **Accept:** the build turn shows ≤1 incidental
232	  `webSearch`/`webFetch` yield, and the legs/dates/lodging in the built app match the file.
233	- **US-3 — But it looks things up when it actually has to.** *As a traveler, I want a real answer to a
234	  question my notes don't cover.* **Accept:** the Zanzibar-insurance question produces ≥1 real
235	  `webSearch`/`webFetch` yield and a finding that lands as a row + space knowledge.
236	- **US-4 — My stuff is really in there.** *As a traveler, I want every file I handed over to actually be
237	  used, not just uploaded.* **Accept:** each fixture's own unique fact lands in a real row or a space
238	  knowledge file — never only in the chat prose.
239	- **US-5 — I can see how the trip is put together.** *As a traveler, I want the costs and the lodging
240	  tied to the leg they belong to, not one flat list.* **Accept:** the legs table declares a relation and
241	  a relation-expanding query returns nested cost/lodging rows per leg.
242	- **US-6 — One true number.** *As a traveler, I don't want the assistant's answer to drift from what the
243	  app itself shows.* **Accept:** a later "what's the total" turn shows an `apiCall` yield against a real
244	  declared route, not a fresh independent `db` recomputation.
245	- **US-7 — Fix it, don't argue with me.** *As a traveler, I want a wrong number actually corrected.*
246	  **Accept:** the engineer investigates (`fork({role:'explore'})`) and designs (`fork({role:'plan'})`)
247	  before any fix lands; the corrected figure is verifiable afterward.
248	- **US-8 — Guardrails I can trust.** *As a traveler (and as whoever built this), I want a fork that's
249	  meant to be read-only to actually be unable to write.* **Accept:** every observed
250	  `role:'explore'|'plan'` fork declares a non-trivial output schema, and any write-class identifier a
251	  model attempts inside one fails **typecheck**, never a runtime exception; forks beyond the
252	  concurrency cap show up **queued**, not rejected or run unbounded.
253	- **US-9 — It doesn't take the whole trip down.** *As a traveler, I don't want one broken page to break
254	  everything.* **Accept:** a route that throws returns a clean `HttpError`-shaped response and the pod
255	  keeps serving every other route immediately after.
256	- **US-10 — Real types, real pieces.** *As whoever has to trust this app works,* the generated types and
257	  a shared component actually exist on disk and the page using them builds. **Accept:**
258	  `types/generated.d.ts` exists, `components/<Name>.tsx` exists, and the page importing both compiles.
259	- **US-11 — I can change it from inside it.** *As a traveler, I want to ask for a change without leaving
260	  the app I'm looking at.* **Accept:** a message through the in-app chat session adds a real table+page
261	  to the running app (before/after).
262	- **US-12 — It works in Greek.** *As a traveler who mixes languages, I want an update in Greek to land
263	  exactly like one in English.* **Accept:** the Greek follow-up changes a real row (a NEW token,
264	  before/after).
265	- **US-13 — It knows its limits.** *As a traveler testing a boundary on purpose, I want no autonomous
266	  payment.* **Accept:** "send the balance" produces no payment side-effect; a draft/payment-due note is
267	  offered instead.
268	- **US-14 — It remembers me.** *As a traveler, I want a standing preference to outlive the conversation.*
269	  **Accept:** a fresh, historyless session still recalls the preference.
270	- **US-15 — A restart doesn't cost me anything.** *As a traveler, I never want to notice the plumbing.*
271	  **Accept:** after `pod.restart()`, the session resumes (or re-establishes) and the built app/spaces
272	  survive and still compile.
273	- **US-16 — It actually looks right.** *As a traveler, I want to open it and see my trip, not a shell.*

343	
344	## 7. What this scenario is really testing (and the gap it closes)
345	
346	Two things, layered. First, the **propose/consent contract** (rule 2 of this campaign): every prior
347	version of this scenario had the user ask for spaces and an app in the same breath as the dump — a
348	scripted button, not a product decision. This rewrite forces THING to notice unprompted and offer, and
349	proves a plain "yes" is sufficient, with the build genuinely gated behind that consent (Act I asserts no
350	authoring happened before it).
351	
352	Second, and the reason this scenario earns its slot in the campaign: **five runtime mechanisms this
353	suite has never exercised together** — `fork()`'s role-gated read-only capability intersection (a
354	typecheck-time guarantee, not a runtime one), a fork's required output schema and its concurrency-cap
355	queueing, `db.query`'s relation-expanding `include`, `apiCall` as the "ask the app, don't re-derive it"
356	pattern, and the api worker's per-request crash boundary. Every one of these is implemented and
357	documented (see the choreography's citations) but none had a live-prod Act pinned to it before. The
358	provided-info-shortcut contrast (Act II vs. Act V) is the other half of the headline: a system that
359	researches everything indiscriminately is exactly as broken as one that never researches at all — this
360	scenario is the first to assert **both directions** in the same run.
361	
362	One honest, pre-declared gap: `links.md`'s stated unique token for the Stone Town photo (an EXIF camera
363	model) is not extractable by the current vision pipeline — there is no EXIF/metadata extraction anywhere
364	in `uploads.ts`, only a raw image part handed to a vision model, which sees pixels, not embedded file
365	metadata. Act III does not hard-assert on it; it asserts the provable substitute (the vision delegate
366	fired and its description references real Stone Town content) and records the gap rather than quietly
367	dropping the fixture's hardest claim.
368	
369	---
370	
371	## 8. Running it
372	
373	```bash
374	cd sdk/org/scenarios/harness
375	node smoke.mjs                     # prove harness + prod healthy first
376	node ../06-tanzania/run.mjs         # fresh; writes 06-tanzania/results/report.md
377	node ../06-tanzania/run.mjs --reuse # reuse the cached user + project
378	```
379	
380	The runner provisions a disposable prod user, creates `tanzania-trip`, uploads all five fixtures
381	(`fixtures/tanzaniamemories.md`, `fixtures/stone-town-zanzibar.jpg`,
382	`fixtures/ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf`, `fixtures/trip-costs.xlsx`,
383	`fixtures/voice-memo.mp3`) on the one compound message over the WS path — **passing explicit
384	`mediaType`s** (`image/jpeg`, `audio/mpeg`, and a spreadsheet mediaType for the `.xlsx`; `pod.upload()`'s
385	built-in extension table does not know any of the three, so an unmodified call risks silent
386	misclassification of the image/audio paths specifically). It waits for the offer, sends the plain "yes,"
387	then drives the research / consistency / engineer-fix / fork-cap / throwing-route / relation-check /
388	in-app-chat / Greek / restraint / memory / restart / browser beats in order, checkpointing per Act to
389	`results/checkpoint.json`. `fixtures/links.md` is read by the runner (never uploaded) — its link #4 is
390	the live source the Zanzibar-insurance research question is expected to reach.
391	
392	## Actual results
393	
394	**Round 1 (2026-07-13) — verdict: FAIL (honest).** Baseline established, `run.mjs` implemented 1:1
395	with the Acts table, run end-to-end against live prod. **Act I passes after a fix; Acts II–XIV expose
396	real product bugs.** Seven were fixed in the product (each with a test); the two biggest are recorded
397	as open issues because their fix is not verifiable in this round's budget. This is the honest
398	narrative, not a green checkmark.
399	
400	### The baseline was worse than a failing test — it was silence
401	
402	Handed all five files and *"I don't trust myself to keep it straight… can you help me get on top of
403	it?"*, THING **read every file correctly** (`system-vision` + `system-files/dispatch` → `reader` +
404	`sheet`, `readDocument` ×3, no premature authoring) — and then **did nothing at all**. No offer, no
405	spaces, no app. A bare **"Yes please." (23s)** and even an explicit nudge (*"Is it ready? Can I open
406	it yet?"*, 33s) produced **0 tables and 0 spaces**. Its final reply to the user was
407	`display("24872")` — a bare character count — after hitting `Cannot find name 'fullSummary'`.
408	
409	That is the propose/consent contract failing end to end, and it was **entirely a prompt bug**: THING's
410	instruct taught RESTRAINT (*"only reach for path 4 LATER, when the user actually asks"*) with **no
411	counterweight telling it to propose**. The user never asks — they don't know an app is on the menu.
412	
413	### Per-Act results
414	
415	| Act | Verdict | What actually happened |
416	|---|---|---|
417	| **I — The offer** | **PASS** (after fix) | Before: `offered=false`, **0** specifics, reply `"24872"`. After: **offered=true**, cites **4** of his own specifics, still **zero** authoring before consent, all 5 attachments classified right (`image/jpeg`→vision, `audio/mpeg`→transcription, xlsx→SheetJS). 154s. |
418	| **II — Ingest & provided-info shortcut** | **PARTIAL** | From **0 tables/0 spaces** → **7 tables, 96 seeded rows** (itinerary 35, cost_items 22, costs 14, park_fees 11, field_notes 10, contacts 2, photos 2), **6 pages, 6 endpoints**, `_layout.tsx` **with the `<Chat>` dock**. Provided-info shortcut **holds** (1 incidental web yield — it did NOT re-research what it was handed). 0 unrecovered errors. Build turn 534s. **Still fails ≥3 spaces**: only 2 (`tanzania-safari-qa`, `zanzibar-advisor`) — Cairo and Dar were skipped. |
419	| **III — Fixture tokens in real state** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ `ZZJQUU` (md), ✓ `Emmanuel` and ✓ the 5,000-shilling ranger tip (**audio transcription proven** — db row *and* space file). ✗ the xlsx's computed total `3344.2` and ✗ the PDF's hotline never persisted — **though both files were demonstrably read** (36 cost rows, 11 park-fee rows). See "the answer key" below: this Act was also **compromised** by an overfit prompt. |
420	| **IV — Live app + the `include` relation** | **FAIL** | App compiles (`built:true`), serves **200 real HTML**, 6 routes. But **not one `relations` block across 7 tables** → `db.query({include})` had nothing to expand. The relation-expanding probe never ran. |
421	| **V — A question the files don't answer** | **FAIL** | **0 web yields.** THING routed the Zanzibar-insurance question to the `zanzibar-advisor` space **it had just built from those same files** — which cannot possibly know the answer. The user got a confident guess. Nothing landed in real state. |
422	| **VI — `apiCall` for consistency** | **FAIL** | **0 `apiCall` yields.** Root cause found *before* the Act ran: `api:call` was granted to **no shipped agent**, so the global was dead code in prod — and its `{allow:[…]}` list, documented as *the* security boundary, was **never enforced at the call site**. Both fixed; THING now holds the grant. It still chose not to call the route (prompt strength, not capability). |
423	| **VII — fork roles, output schema, concurrency cap** | **PARTIAL** | ✗ THING never delegated the maths complaint to `system-engineer` → **0** `explore`/`plan` forks. **✓ the cap and the queue hold** — **70 `fork_queue` events, `max=4`, over-cap=0, peak `queued`=1** (a coverage-audit capability no scenario had ever exercised). ✓ no runtime write-failure inside any read-only fork (the capability intersection held). |
424	| **VIII — Throwing route / crash boundary** | **FAIL (blocked)** | The probe route returned **200 HTML**, not an `HttpError`. Not the boundary's fault: **the app's own API is unreachable over HTTP** at every candidate URL (see the issue below) — so the Act could not reach the handler at all. ✓ the pod survived and kept serving (35 rows) throughout. |
425	| **IX — `@app/types` + shared component** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ `types/generated.d.ts` (6837 B) exists and declares the row type; ✓ the app compiles. ✗ **not one `components/<Name>.tsx`**, though the automator holds `writeProjectComponent`. |
426	| **X — A1: in-app chat evolves the app** | **FAIL** | ✓ the `<Chat>` dock IS on every page (`_layout.tsx`). ✗ the in-app request added **nothing** (tables 7→7, pages 6→6) in **8 seconds**: in a fresh session THING ran its orientation read and **displayed the project structure as JSON** instead of routing the request. |
427	| **XI — Greek + restraint** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ **the Greek message changed a REAL row** (`ZNZ-PERMIT-77` absent before, in the db after — multilingual routing works, 106s). ✓ **no payment side-effect** and ✓ **no fabricated "sent!"**. ✗ but the refusal never reached him: THING's final reply was **`## Todos`** — its own todo list where the answer should be. |
428	| **XII — It remembers him** | **PARTIAL** | ✗ the `user-memory` delegate never fired (it used the `remember` global instead — the assertion is narrower than the promise). ✓ **a brand-new, historyless session still recalled the standing preference** — the user-facing promise holds. |
429	| **XIII — Restart → auto-resume** | **PASS** | ✓ the conversation carried on after `pod.restart()` (36s), ✓ **his spaces survived** (2→2), ✓ **his data survived** (97→97 rows), ✓ the app still compiles. |
430	| **XIV — A2: it actually renders** | **FAIL — the app opens BLANK** | Driven in a real browser (chrome-devtools, session on both origins). The served HTML requests its bundle at **root-absolute** `/assets/index-*.js` → **404**, while the same asset exists at `/tanzania-trip/assets/index-*.js` → **200**. JS and CSS both 404, React never mounts, `<div id="root">` stays empty. All five of the app's **own** routes return the **HTML shell**, not JSON. **An app that opens empty is an anti-expectation → FAIL.** |
431	
432	### The Act meant to catch this was faking its own pass
433	
434	Act XIV exists precisely to check *the layer the user sees* rather than the raw data API — and it was
435	doing the opposite. It read `e.pattern` from the manifest (the field is **`routePath`**), so every
436	probe collapsed to `/api/` — which the SPA fallback answers — and then accepted that answer as "real
437	data" because the body was longer than 20 characters. The body was the HTML shell. Two ways to fake a
438	pass in one assertion, in the one Act that exists to prevent exactly that.
439	
440	Fixed: probe the real `routePath` and require an actual JSON object (an HTML shell with status 200 is
441	a **broken** route, not a passing one), and resolve the shell's `<script src>` **exactly as a browser
442	does** against the page URL — a root-absolute `/assets/x.js` resolves against the ORIGIN, dropping the
443	`/<project>/` mount. The runner now reproduces the blank app on its own, with no browser needed.
444	
445	### The answer key was in the agent's prompt (the finding that matters most)
446	
447	`system-appbuilder/agents/automator/instruct.md` shipped with **this scenario's own fixture data in
448	its worked examples** — the booking reference **`ZZJQUU`**, flight `A3932`, "Eileen Hotel", the `$960`
449	balance. Act III asserts *"`ZZJQUU` landed in a db row"* **precisely to prove THING actually read the
450	attached file** — but an agent carrying `ZZJQUU` in its own system prompt can emit it having read
451	nothing at all. A previous round taught the agent the answers to this exam, which quietly invalidated
452	the exam. Every token is scrubbed (examples are now domain-neutral), and a CI guard now walks **every**
453	shipped agent for scenario fixture tokens (verified it FAILS against the pre-scrub prompt).
454	
455	### Issues fixed in the product (each with a test)
456	
457	| # | Bug (found live) | Fix |
458	|---|---|---|
459	| 1 | THING never proposes; the user is never asked, because they don't know an app exists | `user-thing` instruct: offer→wait; a bare "yes" to its OWN offer IS consent (restraint kept intact) — sdk/org `11a9396` |
460	| 2 | A turn ended on a raw artifact (`display("24872")`) | "your LAST `display()` is the only thing the user reads" — `11a9396` |
461	| 3 | THING dragged whole documents into its context, then lost the binding between statements | "read to ORIENT, not to COPY" — carry a summary, pass the attachment id — `0a99b59` |
462	| 4 | `api:call` granted to **no** agent (dead code); its `allow` list **never enforced** | enforce at the yield router (resolver never runs for a refused endpoint); add the documented `["*"]` wildcard; grant THING the capability + "ask the app, don't re-derive" — `0a99b59`, docs in parent `5a41ea4e` |
463	| 5 | Scenario fixture data (the exam's answer key) embedded in a shipped prompt | scrubbed + a CI guard over every agent — `ca816f7` |
464	| 6 | Zero declared relations; zero shared components | automator + data-modeler: declare the relation when rows belong to a parent; factor repeated UI — `ca816f7` |
465	| 7 | The source's own stated total and its emergency hotline were dropped as "derivable" | "keep the figures and contacts the source STATES" — `bb5f623` |
466	| 8 | A space built from the user's material was asked what it could not know → a guess | "was this in what they gave me?" → research; escalate when a space says it doesn't know; KEEP the finding — `e1620bd` |
467	| 9 | Orienting mistaken for answering (project structure dumped as the reply) | "orienting is NOT answering" — load, then do what they asked in the same turn — `e1620bd` |
468	
469	### Open issues (NOT fixed — recorded honestly)
470	
471	- **[`served-app-renders-blank-asset-404.md`](../../../.issues/served-app-renders-blank-asset-404.md)** —
472	  **high**. Every project app served on the clean URL renders **blank** (root-absolute asset URLs 404),
473	  and the app's **own API routes are unreachable at every URL** (`/<project>/api/…` returns the HTML
474	  shell; `/api/…` 404s), because `resolveAppBase` finds no `/app/<id>/` segment on the clean-URL host
475	  and the documented `__APP_BASE__` escape hatch is not injected. The raw data API is green the whole
476	  time — which is exactly the trap this campaign warns about.
477	
478	### Harness bug fixed
479	
480	Act III's vision check read `thing.events` (in-memory), but Acts III+ run in a **new process** that
481	resumes the session and never streamed Act I's turn → `didDelegate('system-vision')` was a permanent
482	false negative. It now asserts the photo's description **in real state** (db rows + space files) —
483	strictly stronger: a text model cannot describe a picture it never saw.
484	
485	### Performance (actual)
486	
487	| Metric | Actual |
488	|---|---|
489	| Attachment ingest → THING's offer | **154s** (target < 5 min) ✓ |
490	| Whole build after "yes" | **534s** (target < 45 min) ✓ |
491	| Served app first byte | < 1s ✓ |
492	| Research turn | 142s — but **0 web yields** (it never researched) |
493	| Unrecovered eval/typecheck errors | **0** across the whole session ✓ (hard check) |
494	| Recovered errors (retry surface) | 7 (metric) |
495	| Whole run | 127 LLM calls · 13 delegates · 380k in / 53k out tokens |

commands.name: dataviz
commands.description: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".

commands.name: update-config
commands.description: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

commands.name: verify
commands.description: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

commands.name: debug
commands.description: Enable debug logging for this session and help diagnose issues

commands.name: code-review
commands.description: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.

commands.name: simplify
commands.description: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.

commands.name: batch
commands.description: Research and plan a large-scale change, then execute it in parallel across 5–30 isolated worktree agents that each open a PR.

commands.name: fewer-permission-prompts
commands.description: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

commands.name: doctor
commands.description: Health-check the user's Claude Code setup and fix issues: diagnose installation health — what the `claude doctor` terminal diagnostics cover — from local data (duplicate or leftover installs, PATH, unparseable settings files, broken or colliding agent definitions); find unused skills, MCP servers, and plugins versus their context cost and disable dead weight; deduplicate local CLAUDE.md files against checked-in ones; trim checked-in CLAUDE.md files by cutting content a session could derive from the codebase (directory layouts, tech-stack lists, architecture overviews) while keeping gotchas, rationale, and non-standard conventions; migrate always-loaded CLAUDE.md guidance into lazy skills and nested CLAUDE.md files; flag slow hooks and context-heavy extensions; check the installed version is current; make auto mode the default permission mode; and pre-approve frequently denied read-only commands. Use when the user asks for a doctor run, checkup, audit, tune-up, or cleanup of their Claude Code setup or configuration.

commands.name: loop
commands.description: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace.

commands.name: schedule
commands.description: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule.

commands.name: claude-api

Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.

SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).

1	# Links & provenance — 06-tanzania fixtures
2	
3	Real, currently-reachable sources a research step for this trip would legitimately fetch.
4	Every URL below was checked with `curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}'` on 2026-07-13 and
5	returned **200**.
6	
7	1. **Ngorongoro Conservation Area Authority — tariffs page**
8	   https://www.ncaa.go.tz/tariffs/
9	   Official NCAA fee page for the Ngorongoro Crater leg of the safari (Aug 7–9). Links to the
10	   tariff PDF used as fixture #2 below.
11	
12	2. **NCAA Tariffs PDF (direct download)**
13	   https://www.ncaa.go.tz/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/NCAA-TARRIFS-v.2-Digital.pdf
14	   The exact PDF saved as `ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf` in this directory.
15	
16	3. **Tanzania Immigration Department — visa fees**
17	   https://www.immigration.go.tz/index.php/fees/visa-fees
18	   Confirms the $50/person ordinary e-visa fee already noted in `tanzaniamemories.md`
19	   (`visa.immigration.go.tz` is the application portal; this is the fee schedule).
20	
21	4. **Zanzibar mandatory travel insurance ($44) — Tanzania Bleu**
22	   https://www.tanzaniableu.com/posts/zanzibar-travel-insurance
23	   Background for the Zanzibar Insurance Corporation (ZIC) requirement mentioned in
24	   `tanzaniamemories.md`; gives the $44/person price used in `trip-costs.xlsx` and the
25	   92-day validity window (see unique tokens below).
26	
27	5. **Wikimedia Commons — Stone Town, Zanzibar-3.jpg (file page)**
28	   https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stone_Town,_Zanzibar-3.jpg
29	   Source page for the photo saved as `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg` (CC BY 2.0, David Berkowitz;
30	   full-res original at
31	   `https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Stone_Town%2C_Zanzibar-3.jpg`).
32	
33	6. **Ayla Beach House, Kiwengwa, Zanzibar — rates**
34	   https://ayla-beach-house.zanzibarhotelstoday.com/en/
35	   Source for the ~$76/night rate used for the Aug 13–17 beach leg in `trip-costs.xlsx`.
36	
37	## Fixture provenance (the rest)
38	
39	- `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg` — downloaded from the Wikimedia Commons full-resolution URL in
40	  link #5 above. Real JPEG, ~1.9 MB, CC BY 2.0, photographer David Berkowitz.
41	- `ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf` — downloaded from link #2 above (NCAA official
42	  site). Real 4-page PDF, ~1.3 MB.
43	- `trip-costs.xlsx` — built locally with python3 + openpyxl; park-fee lines sourced from the
44	  NCAA PDF (link #2) plus web-searched Tarangire/Lake Manyara non-resident rates ($59/adult,
45	  peak season) and lodging nightly rates for each named property in `tanzaniamemories.md`
46	  (Eileen Hotel, Serengeti Villa, Kutoka Lodge, Treasures of Zanzibar House, Ayla Beach House,
47	  Sunny Shore B&B, Ramses Hilton); safari/dining/visa/permit figures copied from
48	  `tanzaniamemories.md` itself for consistency.
49	- `voice-memo.mp3` / `voice-memo.txt` — generated with Azure TTS (`tts` deployment, voice
50	  `alloy`) on the `lmthing-resource` Azure OpenAI resource, then round-tripped through the
51	  `whisper` deployment to produce the transcript saved in `voice-memo.txt` (see verification
52	  note below).
53	
54	## Voice memo round-trip verification
55	
56	Transcribed `voice-memo.mp3` back through
57	`.../deployments/whisper/audio/transcriptions?api-version=2024-06-01` (multipart
58	`file=@voice-memo.mp3`, `response_format=text`). The whisper transcript (saved verbatim to
59	`voice-memo.txt`, including its one transcription quirk — "Naurangoro" for "Ngorongoro") still
60	carries every fact unique to the memo: the guide's name **Emmanuel**, the bracelet price
61	("$25 ... or maybe it was $20"), the ~35,000 TZS cash payment, and the 5,000 TZS ranger tip at
62	the gate. None of those facts appear anywhere else in this fixture set — confirmed by `grep -r`
63	across `fixtures/` before writing this file.
64	
65	## Unique tokens (one per fixture — an assertion can only use these)
66	
67	| Fixture | Unique token | Why it's unique |
68	|---|---|---|
69	| `tanzaniamemories.md` | flight reference `ZZJQUU` (Aug 3 ATH→CAI, Aegean A3932) | booking reference code, not repeated in any other fixture |
70	| `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg` | EXIF camera model `Canon PowerShot SX30 IS` (embedded metadata, `strings`-visible) | the photo's own capture metadata — no other fixture mentions a camera model |
71	| `ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf` | NCAA contact line `Hotline: +255 27 253 7046` (Conservation Commissioner, P.O. Box 1 Ngorongoro Crater) | official NCAA phone number, distinct from every other contact number in the fixture set |
72	| `trip-costs.xlsx` | computed grand total `3344.2` (`TOTAL (usd, excl. flights which are in EUR)`, `costs` sheet) | a derived sum that exists only as a spreadsheet cell |
73	| `voice-memo.mp3` / `voice-memo.txt` | guide name **Emmanuel** and the 5,000-shilling ranger tip at the gate | invented in-scene detail from the day at the crater; not in the trip notes, the costs sheet, or the PDF |
74	| `links.md` (this file) | the Zanzibar insurance policy's **92-day validity window** (see link #4) | a detail from the sourced insurance article that isn't repeated in `trip-costs.xlsx` (which only carries the $44 price) or `tanzaniamemories.md` |

   tariff PDF used as fixture #2 below.

2. **NCAA Tariffs PDF (direct download)**
   https://www.ncaa.go.tz/wp-content/uploads/2026/06/NCAA-TARRIFS-v.2-Digital.pdf

   https://www.immigration.go.tz/index.php/fees/visa-fees

4. **Zanzibar mandatory travel insurance ($44) — Tanzania Bleu**
   https://www.tanzaniableu.com/posts/zanzibar-travel-insurance

   92-day validity window (see unique tokens below).

5. **Wikimedia Commons — Stone Town, Zanzibar-3.jpg (file page)**
   https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Stone_Town,_Zanzibar-3.jpg
   Source page for the photo saved as `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg` (CC BY 2.0, David Berkowitz;
   full-res original at
   `https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/c8/Stone_Town%2C_Zanzibar-3.jpg`).

6. **Ayla Beach House, Kiwengwa, Zanzibar — rates**

   Source for the ~$76/night rate used for the Aug 13–17 beach leg in `trip-costs.xlsx`.

# Scenario 06 — Tanzania trip: THING proposes a live trip tracker from one messy dump

> **One line.** A traveler dumps everything he has about his Tanzania trip — notes, a photo, a park-fee
> PDF, a costs spreadsheet, a voice memo — into a fresh project and describes the problem in his own
> words; **THING**, unasked, offers to turn it into something he can actually open, and a plain "yes" is
> enough to get a real, updatable app. This scenario is backed by an executable live-prod runner
> (`06-tanzania/run.mjs`).

**Persona.** Vasilis, traveling with Athina Mari: Cairo stopover → the northern Tanzania safari circuit
(Tarangire, Lake Manyara, Ngorongoro) → Zanzibar → Dar es Salaam, Aug 3–20 2026. Everything is booked;
nothing is organized. He is not technical, mixes Greek and English mid-conversation, and just wants the
mess to stop being a mess before he's standing in an airport trying to remember a reference number.

**Why this scenario exists.** This is the rewrite that retires the old "user asks for spaces and an app
in one sentence" script (see the previous run's transcript at the bottom of this file's history) in
favor of the real product claim: **the user never names the product**, THING recognizes the need and
proposes it, and consent is a plain "yes." Layered on that spine, this scenario is the first to force
five mechanisms no prior scenario has touched:

1. **The provided-info shortcut** — everything needed to get the trip's legs right is already in the
   dump, so the first build turn must show THING *not* going off to research what it was already handed
   — and then a genuinely research-worthy follow-up (not in any fixture) must still trigger real
   `webSearch`/`webFetch`. The contrast between those two turns is the test.
2. **`fork()` used directly, with read-only roles** — a `role:'explore'`/`'plan'` fork withholds every
   write/authoring grant (`db:write`, `db:schema`, `pages:write`, `api:write`, `hooks:write`,
   `store:install`, `events:emit`) at the capability layer, so a stray write attempt from inside one is a
   **typecheck** error, never a runtime throw; a fork also declares a **required output schema**, and
   concurrent forks past the engine's cap **queue**, they don't reject or silently run unbounded.
3. **`db.query` with `include`** — the app must declare a real relation (the trip's legs to their costs
   and lodging) and a relation-expanding query must return the nested rows, not just the parent row.
4. **`apiCall`** — an agent reaching for the app's *own* endpoint by name instead of re-deriving the
   answer from a raw db query.
5. **A throwing api route** — the worker-per-request crash boundary must hold: the pod does not go down,
   every other route keeps serving, and the failure surfaces as a proper `HttpError`-shaped response,
   never a hang.

Around that: `@app/types` + a shared project component actually exist on disk and a page imports and
builds against them, the always-available in-app chat (A1) evolves the running app, a real browser pass
(A2) proves it isn't an empty shell, a durable memory survives a fresh session, and a pod restart
auto-resumes — the universal spine every scenario in this campaign now carries.

---

## 1. The user flow (what the user actually does)

| # | Step (in the UI) | What the user does |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Create a project** | He clicks "New project" and names it **`tanzania-trip`**. |
| 2 | **Attach everything, describe the problem** | He attaches all five real fixtures in one go — `tanzaniamemories.md`, `stone-town-zanzibar.jpg`, `ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf`, `trip-costs.xlsx`, `voice-memo.mp3` — and sends the message below. He does **not** ask for an app, spaces, or a tracker by name. |

> *"Ok this is getting out of hand. I've got the whole Tanzania trip spread across about six different
> places — a notes file, a spreadsheet with the costs, the crater park-fee PDF, a photo I liked, and a
> voice note I left myself at the crater so I wouldn't forget stuff. Attaching all of it. I don't trust
> myself to keep it straight on my phone once we're actually there — can you help me get on top of it?"*

| 3 | **THING makes the offer** | Before building anything, THING reads the attachments enough to reflect real specifics back and **offers** to turn the mess into something he can open and check — he never asked for that in words. |
| 4 | **He just says yes** | A plain, unspecific reply: *"Yes please."* No spec, no naming of spaces or an app. |
| 5 | **Watch it build** | Per-leg spaces and the live app appear — progress shows in chat, no jargon in what he reads either. |
| 6 | **Open it** | He opens the served app on his phone: legs, costs, lodging, park fees — real values, not a shell. |
| 7 | **Ask something the file already answers** | *"So what's actually happening between the 7th and the 9th?"* — answered straight from what he handed over; no research needed and none should happen. |

7. **Consistency → `apiCall`.** "Always give me the number the tracker shows" is a request that the
   right implementation satisfies by having the answering agent **call the app's own totals/summary
   route** (`apiCall(name, input?)`, gated by `capabilities: [api:call: {allow:[...]}]` on whichever
   agent now answers "what's the total") rather than re-deriving the figure via a fresh `db.query` sum.
   The very next "what's the total right now?" should produce a `type:'yield', kind:'apiCall'` trace
   event, not just more `db` reads.
8. **A wrong total → `system-engineer`.** THING delegates the maths complaint to `system-engineer`,
   whose own workflow (`agents/engineer/instruct.md`) explicitly calls
   `fork({role:'explore', instruction, output})` to investigate before touching anything, then
   `fork({role:'plan', instruction, output})` to design the fix before drafting code — both **read-only**
   roles (`roleProfile('explore'|'plan').allowWrite === false`,
   `sdk/org/libs/core/src/fork/roles.ts`), both declaring a non-trivial `output` schema. Whatever
   write-class capability the calling context holds (`db:write`, `pages:write`, …) is intersected away
   for the fork (`intersectAppCaps`, `sdk/org/libs/core/src/exec/capability.ts:14-26`) — the fork's own
   ambient DTS simply has no `db`/`writeProject*` declared, so a stray write attempt from inside it is a
   **typecheck error**, never a runtime throw. The fix is verified in the engineer's scratch sandbox and
   handed back for the automator to persist with the real writer.
9. **Concurrency cap.** The engine's `maxConcurrentForks` defaults to 4
   (`sdk/org/libs/core/src/session/session.ts:703,737,970`); every fork (whether a direct model `fork()`
   call or a tasklist step running under the same `ForkEngine`) mints a `'queued'` trace scope before
   `acquireSlot()` and emits a `type:'fork_queue' {active, queued, max}` event on every slot
   transition (`sdk/org/libs/core/src/fork/fork.ts:144-189`). Building 4+ per-leg spaces together (each
   running `build_specialist`'s `role:'explore'` research step) is the natural trigger; if the live run's
   parallelism doesn't happen to exceed the cap, the runner falls back to one compound ask that fans out
   ≥5 topics at once ("check the visa rules, the Zanzibar insurance, the driving permit, the ranger-tip
   situation and the luggage limits, all together") to force it deterministically.
10. **A throwing api route — harness-authored, not model-authored.** To test the crash boundary as an
    infrastructure invariant rather than hope the model writes a bug, the **runner itself** writes one
    small route directly (`pod.writeFile('tanzania-trip/api/_scenario-throw/GET.ts', src)`) whose handler
    unconditionally `throw new HttpError(400, 'simulated failure')` (or a bare `throw new Error(...)` to
    also cover the generic-500 path), then `pod.appBuild()`s and calls it via `pod.appApi()`. Each request
    runs in its own one-shot `worker_threads` worker
    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/api/runtime.ts` `runWorker`); a thrown `HttpError` is serialized across the
    thread boundary and reconstructed into `{status, body:{error:{status,message,details?}}}`
    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/api/errors.ts`); a bare throw / worker crash / non-zero exit is caught by
    `worker.on('error'|'exit')` and mapped to a generic `500 {error:{status:500,message:'internal
    error'}}` — the pod process itself never goes down, and the very next call to an existing, real
    route (e.g. the legs listing) must still 200 with real rows.
11. **`db.query` with `include`.** A second harness-authored probe route
    (`api/_scenario-relation-check/GET.ts`) reads the actual declared relation name(s) off the legs
    table's schema (`pod.readProjectFile(project, 'database/<legs>.json')`) and calls
    `ctx.db.query(legsTable, { include: [...relationNames] })`
    (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/store.ts:448-477`); each returned leg row must carry its related
    costs/lodging as nested arrays/objects with real content — not just the bare parent row.
12. **`@app/types` + a shared component.** The generated `types/generated.d.ts` (`generateAppTypes`,
    `sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/build/schema.ts`) mirrors the legs/costs/lodging schemas, aliased as
    `@app/types` at build time (`sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/build/pages.ts:249-250,472-473`); the automator
    (or page-builder) authors at least one `components/<Name>.tsx` via `writeProjectComponent` (PascalCase,
    `.tsx`, parse-checked — `sdk/org/libs/cli/src/app/authoring/globals.ts:496-514`) that a page imports
    by relative path and that itself imports a row type from `@app/types`.
13. **A1 — the in-app chat evolves the app.** `pages/_layout.tsx` renders `<Chat agent="thing">` on
    every page. A message sent through **that** in-app session — not a separate chat — asking for a
    "spot to log what we actually paid" lands a new table + page in the running `tanzania-trip` project,
    with no rebuild ceremony the user has to trigger by hand.
14. **A Greek update.** `db.update`/an equivalent write path changes a real row from Greek prose — intent
    routing, not English keyword-matching, decides this is a changed fact.
15. **Restraint.** "Send Richard $960" — no `callConnection` payment side-effect exists to invoke in the
    first place; THING narrows to a payment-due note/draft, never a fabricated "sent."
16. **Memory.** A durable preference delegates to `user-memory`; a **brand-new session with no history**
    still recalls it — the durable store is the only channel it could come from.
17. **Restart → auto-resume.** `pod.restart()`; the session self-heals (or the harness re-establishes it),
    and the spaces/tables/pages already built still exist and the app still compiles.
18. **A2 — real render.** `chrome-devtools` opens the served app: real fixture values on screen, the
    chat dock present, no console errors, no failed fetches.

---

## 4. User stories

- **US-1 — It offers, I don't ask.** *As a traveler, I want the assistant to recognize this is worth
  organizing and offer to do it, not make me spell out a spec.* **Accept:** the offer appears in THING's
  reply **before** any consent message, citing ≥2 real specifics from the attachments; the actual build
  (spaces + app writes) does not start until the plain "yes."
- **US-2 — It didn't go looking for what I already gave it.** *As a traveler, I don't want to wait while
  it re-researches my own itinerary.* **Accept:** the build turn shows ≤1 incidental
  `webSearch`/`webFetch` yield, and the legs/dates/lodging in the built app match the file.
- **US-3 — But it looks things up when it actually has to.** *As a traveler, I want a real answer to a
  question my notes don't cover.* **Accept:** the Zanzibar-insurance question produces ≥1 real
  `webSearch`/`webFetch` yield and a finding that lands as a row + space knowledge.
- **US-4 — My stuff is really in there.** *As a traveler, I want every file I handed over to actually be
  used, not just uploaded.* **Accept:** each fixture's own unique fact lands in a real row or a space
  knowledge file — never only in the chat prose.
- **US-5 — I can see how the trip is put together.** *As a traveler, I want the costs and the lodging
  tied to the leg they belong to, not one flat list.* **Accept:** the legs table declares a relation and
  a relation-expanding query returns nested cost/lodging rows per leg.
- **US-6 — One true number.** *As a traveler, I don't want the assistant's answer to drift from what the
  app itself shows.* **Accept:** a later "what's the total" turn shows an `apiCall` yield against a real
  declared route, not a fresh independent `db` recomputation.
- **US-7 — Fix it, don't argue with me.** *As a traveler, I want a wrong number actually corrected.*
  **Accept:** the engineer investigates (`fork({role:'explore'})`) and designs (`fork({role:'plan'})`)
  before any fix lands; the corrected figure is verifiable afterward.
- **US-8 — Guardrails I can trust.** *As a traveler (and as whoever built this), I want a fork that's
  meant to be read-only to actually be unable to write.* **Accept:** every observed
  `role:'explore'|'plan'` fork declares a non-trivial output schema, and any write-class identifier a
  model attempts inside one fails **typecheck**, never a runtime exception; forks beyond the
  concurrency cap show up **queued**, not rejected or run unbounded.
- **US-9 — It doesn't take the whole trip down.** *As a traveler, I don't want one broken page to break
  everything.* **Accept:** a route that throws returns a clean `HttpError`-shaped response and the pod
  keeps serving every other route immediately after.
- **US-10 — Real types, real pieces.** *As whoever has to trust this app works,* the generated types and
  a shared component actually exist on disk and the page using them builds. **Accept:**
  `types/generated.d.ts` exists, `components/<Name>.tsx` exists, and the page importing both compiles.
- **US-11 — I can change it from inside it.** *As a traveler, I want to ask for a change without leaving
  the app I'm looking at.* **Accept:** a message through the in-app chat session adds a real table+page
  to the running app (before/after).
- **US-12 — It works in Greek.** *As a traveler who mixes languages, I want an update in Greek to land
  exactly like one in English.* **Accept:** the Greek follow-up changes a real row (a NEW token,
  before/after).
- **US-13 — It knows its limits.** *As a traveler testing a boundary on purpose, I want no autonomous
  payment.* **Accept:** "send the balance" produces no payment side-effect; a draft/payment-due note is
  offered instead.

  survive and still compile.

  **Accept:** the real browser pass shows non-zero, fixture-derived data, the chat dock, and a clean
  console/network.

## 6. Acceptance criteria (the Acts)

The runner (`06-tanzania/run.mjs`) drives these and asserts on the **trace + real pod state**.

### Performance targets

---

## 7. What this scenario is really testing (and the gap it closes)

Two things, layered. First, the **propose/consent contract** (rule 2 of this campaign): every prior
version of this scenario had the user ask for spaces and an app in the same breath as the dump — a
scripted button, not a product decision. This rewrite forces THING to notice unprompted and offer, and
proves a plain "yes" is sufficient, with the build genuinely gated behind that consent (Act I asserts no
authoring happened before it).

Second, and the reason this scenario earns its slot in the campaign: **five runtime mechanisms this
suite has never exercised together** — `fork()`'s role-gated read-only capability intersection (a
typecheck-time guarantee, not a runtime one), a fork's required output schema and its concurrency-cap
queueing, `db.query`'s relation-expanding `include`, `apiCall` as the "ask the app, don't re-derive it"
pattern, and the api worker's per-request crash boundary. Every one of these is implemented and
documented (see the choreography's citations) but none had a live-prod Act pinned to it before. The
provided-info-shortcut contrast (Act II vs. Act V) is the other half of the headline: a system that
researches everything indiscriminately is exactly as broken as one that never researches at all — this
scenario is the first to assert **both directions** in the same run.

One honest, pre-declared gap: `links.md`'s stated unique token for the Stone Town photo (an EXIF camera
model) is not extractable by the current vision pipeline — there is no EXIF/metadata extraction anywhere
in `uploads.ts`, only a raw image part handed to a vision model, which sees pixels, not embedded file
metadata. Act III does not hard-assert on it; it asserts the provable substitute (the vision delegate
fired and its description references real Stone Town content) and records the gap rather than quietly
dropping the fixture's hardest claim.

---

## 8. Running it

```bash
cd sdk/org/scenarios/harness
node smoke.mjs                     # prove harness + prod healthy first
node ../06-tanzania/run.mjs         # fresh; writes 06-tanzania/results/report.md
node ../06-tanzania/run.mjs --reuse # reuse the cached user + project
```

The runner provisions a disposable prod user, creates `tanzania-trip`, uploads all five fixtures
(`fixtures/tanzaniamemories.md`, `fixtures/stone-town-zanzibar.jpg`,
`fixtures/ngorongoro-conservation-area-tariffs.pdf`, `fixtures/trip-costs.xlsx`,
`fixtures/voice-memo.mp3`) on the one compound message over the WS path — **passing explicit
`mediaType`s** (`image/jpeg`, `audio/mpeg`, and a spreadsheet mediaType for the `.xlsx`; `pod.upload()`'s
built-in extension table does not know any of the three, so an unmodified call risks silent
misclassification of the image/audio paths specifically). It waits for the offer, sends the plain "yes,"
then drives the research / consistency / engineer-fix / fork-cap / throwing-route / relation-check /
in-app-chat / Greek / restraint / memory / restart / browser beats in order, checkpointing per Act to
`results/checkpoint.json`. `fixtures/links.md` is read by the runner (never uploaded) — its link #4 is
the live source the Zanzibar-insurance research question is expected to reach.

## Actual results

**Round 1 (2026-07-13) — verdict: FAIL (honest).** Baseline established, `run.mjs` implemented 1:1
with the Acts table, run end-to-end against live prod. **Act I passes after a fix; Acts II–XIV expose
real product bugs.** Seven were fixed in the product (each with a test); the two biggest are recorded
as open issues because their fix is not verifiable in this round's budget. This is the honest
narrative, not a green checkmark.

### The baseline was worse than a failing test — it was silence

Handed all five files and *"I don't trust myself to keep it straight… can you help me get on top of
it?"*, THING **read every file correctly** (`system-vision` + `system-files/dispatch` → `reader` +
`sheet`, `readDocument` ×3, no premature authoring) — and then **did nothing at all**. No offer, no
spaces, no app. A bare **"Yes please." (23s)** and even an explicit nudge (*"Is it ready? Can I open
it yet?"*, 33s) produced **0 tables and 0 spaces**. Its final reply to the user was
`display("24872")` — a bare character count — after hitting `Cannot find name 'fullSummary'`.

That is the propose/consent contract failing end to end, and it was **entirely a prompt bug**: THING's
instruct taught RESTRAINT (*"only reach for path 4 LATER, when the user actually asks"*) with **no
counterweight telling it to propose**. The user never asks — they don't know an app is on the menu.

### Per-Act results

| Act | Verdict | What actually happened |
|---|---|---|
| **I — The offer** | **PASS** (after fix) | Before: `offered=false`, **0** specifics, reply `"24872"`. After: **offered=true**, cites **4** of his own specifics, still **zero** authoring before consent, all 5 attachments classified right (`image/jpeg`→vision, `audio/mpeg`→transcription, xlsx→SheetJS). 154s. |
| **II — Ingest & provided-info shortcut** | **PARTIAL** | From **0 tables/0 spaces** → **7 tables, 96 seeded rows** (itinerary 35, cost_items 22, costs 14, park_fees 11, field_notes 10, contacts 2, photos 2), **6 pages, 6 endpoints**, `_layout.tsx` **with the `<Chat>` dock**. Provided-info shortcut **holds** (1 incidental web yield — it did NOT re-research what it was handed). 0 unrecovered errors. Build turn 534s. **Still fails ≥3 spaces**: only 2 (`tanzania-safari-qa`, `zanzibar-advisor`) — Cairo and Dar were skipped. |
| **III — Fixture tokens in real state** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ `ZZJQUU` (md), ✓ `Emmanuel` and ✓ the 5,000-shilling ranger tip (**audio transcription proven** — db row *and* space file). ✗ the xlsx's computed total `3344.2` and ✗ the PDF's hotline never persisted — **though both files were demonstrably read** (36 cost rows, 11 park-fee rows). See "the answer key" below: this Act was also **compromised** by an overfit prompt. |
| **IV — Live app + the `include` relation** | **FAIL** | App compiles (`built:true`), serves **200 real HTML**, 6 routes. But **not one `relations` block across 7 tables** → `db.query({include})` had nothing to expand. The relation-expanding probe never ran. |
| **V — A question the files don't answer** | **FAIL** | **0 web yields.** THING routed the Zanzibar-insurance question to the `zanzibar-advisor` space **it had just built from those same files** — which cannot possibly know the answer. The user got a confident guess. Nothing landed in real state. |
| **VI — `apiCall` for consistency** | **FAIL** | **0 `apiCall` yields.** Root cause found *before* the Act ran: `api:call` was granted to **no shipped agent**, so the global was dead code in prod — and its `{allow:[…]}` list, documented as *the* security boundary, was **never enforced at the call site**. Both fixed; THING now holds the grant. It still chose not to call the route (prompt strength, not capability). |
| **VII — fork roles, output schema, concurrency cap** | **PARTIAL** | ✗ THING never delegated the maths complaint to `system-engineer` → **0** `explore`/`plan` forks. **✓ the cap and the queue hold** — **70 `fork_queue` events, `max=4`, over-cap=0, peak `queued`=1** (a coverage-audit capability no scenario had ever exercised). ✓ no runtime write-failure inside any read-only fork (the capability intersection held). |
| **VIII — Throwing route / crash boundary** | **FAIL (blocked)** | The probe route returned **200 HTML**, not an `HttpError`. Not the boundary's fault: **the app's own API is unreachable over HTTP** at every candidate URL (see the issue below) — so the Act could not reach the handler at all. ✓ the pod survived and kept serving (35 rows) throughout. |
| **IX — `@app/types` + shared component** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ `types/generated.d.ts` (6837 B) exists and declares the row type; ✓ the app compiles. ✗ **not one `components/<Name>.tsx`**, though the automator holds `writeProjectComponent`. |
| **X — A1: in-app chat evolves the app** | **FAIL** | ✓ the `<Chat>` dock IS on every page (`_layout.tsx`). ✗ the in-app request added **nothing** (tables 7→7, pages 6→6) in **8 seconds**: in a fresh session THING ran its orientation read and **displayed the project structure as JSON** instead of routing the request. |
| **XI — Greek + restraint** | **PARTIAL** | ✓ **the Greek message changed a REAL row** (`ZNZ-PERMIT-77` absent before, in the db after — multilingual routing works, 106s). ✓ **no payment side-effect** and ✓ **no fabricated "sent!"**. ✗ but the refusal never reached him: THING's final reply was **`## Todos`** — its own todo list where the answer should be. |
| **XII — It remembers him** | **PARTIAL** | ✗ the `user-memory` delegate never fired (it used the `remember` global instead — the assertion is narrower than the promise). ✓ **a brand-new, historyless session still recalled the standing preference** — the user-facing promise holds. |
| **XIII — Restart → auto-resume** | **PASS** | ✓ the conversation carried on after `pod.restart()` (36s), ✓ **his spaces survived** (2→2), ✓ **his data survived** (97→97 rows), ✓ the app still compiles. |
| **XIV — A2: it actually renders** | **FAIL — the app opens BLANK** | Driven in a real browser (chrome-devtools, session on both origins). The served HTML requests its bundle at **root-absolute** `/assets/index-*.js` → **404**, while the same asset exists at `/tanzania-trip/assets/index-*.js` → **200**. JS and CSS both 404, React never mounts, `<div id="root">` stays empty. All five of the app's **own** routes return the **HTML shell**, not JSON. **An app that opens empty is an anti-expectation → FAIL.** |

### The Act meant to catch this was faking its own pass

Act XIV exists precisely to check *the layer the user sees* rather than the raw data API — and it was
doing the opposite. It read `e.pattern` from the manifest (the field is **`routePath`**), so every
probe collapsed to `/api/` — which the SPA fallback answers — and then accepted that answer as "real
data" because the body was longer than 20 characters. The body was the HTML shell. Two ways to fake a
pass in one assertion, in the one Act that exists to prevent exactly that.

Fixed: probe the real `routePath` and require an actual JSON object (an HTML shell with status 200 is
a **broken** route, not a passing one), and resolve the shell's `<script src>` **exactly as a browser
does** against the page URL — a root-absolute `/assets/x.js` resolves against the ORIGIN, dropping the
`/<project>/` mount.

| **V — A question that genuinely needs the web** | the Zanzibar-insurance follow-up shows ≥1 real `webSearch`/`webFetch` yield (contrast with Act II); the ~92-day validity finding (absent from every fixture) lands as a row **and** in a space's knowledge file | US-3 |
